name: Selenium
contraindications: Avoid chronic high-dose intake above established upper limit due to selenosis risk

sources.title: Selenium Fact Sheet for Health Professionals (NIH ODS)
NIH Cohort Study on Se and Diabetes

description: Selenium is incorporated into selenoproteins involved in oxidative defense and thyroid hormone metabolism. Adult intake guidance is centered on microgram-scale intake, and higher chronic intake raises toxicity risk.
summary: Selenium is an essential trace mineral for antioxidant and thyroid-related selenoproteins; adequacy is important, while chronic excess can cause selenosis.
mechanism: Established with proposed extensions: selenium functions through selenoproteins (including glutathione peroxidase and deiodinase enzymes), supporting oxidative balance and T4-to-T3 conversion.
safetyNotes: Deficiency can contribute to clinically significant problems in low-selenium settings.
Chronic excess intake can cause selenosis (for example garlic breath, hair/nail changes, gastrointestinal symptoms, and neuropathy).
Adult upper intake level context: about 400 micrograms/day.
interactions: May interact with anticoagulant contexts; potential heavy-metal interaction claims are formulation/context dependent and not a routine supplementation rationale.
dosage: Some studies evaluated 200 micrograms/day, but routine use above adequacy targets is not generally established.
Adult upper intake level context: about 400 micrograms/day.
preparation: Common supplemental forms include selenomethionine and inorganic forms such as selenite.
regulatoryStatus: Essential trace nutrient with established dietary reference intakes; permitted in dietary supplement products.
